story_meta.thesis: National wealth explains part of life expectancy, but countries can still significantly overperform or underperform their income level.
story_meta.subhed: This prototype chapter is a narrative shell for the full Pudding-style story build.

chapters.label: Chapter 1
chapters.title: Wealth predicts a lot, but not everything.
chapters.body: Each country is a dot. The x-axis is income per person, and the y-axis is life expectancy. The line is the wealth expectation curve for 2010.

chapters.label: Chapter 2
chapters.title: The United States sits below its wealth expectation.
chapters.body: In 2010, the U.S. life expectancy is below the model-predicted value for countries at a similar income level.

chapters.label: Chapter 3
chapters.title: Several similarly wealthy countries sit higher on outcomes.
chapters.body: Peer countries with comparable income levels tend to land above the U.S. on life expectancy in this snapshot.

chapters.label: Chapter 4
chapters.title: Some countries strongly overperform, others strongly underperform.
chapters.body: Residual z-scores quantify how far countries sit above or below what their income predicts.

chapters.label: Chapter 5
chapters.title: Long-run trend gains are uneven across countries.
chapters.body: From 2006 to 2015, country trajectories diverge widely. Progress is possible at many income levels.
